I am a third year undergraduate student at BITS Pilani. I am a beginner with a developing interest in Systems Software Development, with a strong foundation in C++. I am currently learning GoLang. I am also part of Postman API Lab as an Associate where I work on integrating APIs.
To know more about my work, download my resume.

title: Rohan Tiwari

content: Click the Cite button above to demo the feature to enable visitors to import publication metadata into their reference management software.    Create your slides in Markdown - click the Slides button to check out the example.   Supplementary notes can be added here, including [code, math, and images](https://wowchemy.com/docs/writing-markdown-latex/). --
summary: Simulating physical network paths (e.g., Internet) is a cornerstone research problem in the emerging sub-field of AI-for-networking. We seek a model that generates end-to-end packet delay values in response to the time-varying load offered by a sender, which is typically a function of the previously output delays. We formulate an ML problem at the intersection of dynamical systems, sequential decision making, and time-series generative modeling. We propose a novel grey-box approach to network simulation that embeds the semantics of physical network path in a new RNN-style architecture called Recurrent Buffering Unit, providing the interpretability of standard network simulator tools, the power of neural models, the efficiency of SGD-based techniques for learning, and yielding promising results on synthetic and real-world network traces.
title: Simulating Network Paths with Recurrent Buffering Units

Supplementary notes can be added here, including [code, math, and images](https://wowchemy.com/docs/writing-markdown-latex/). --
summary: While network simulation is widely used for evaluating network protocols and applications, ensuring realism remains a key challenge. There has been much work on simulating network mechanisms faithfully (e.g., links, buffers, etc.), but less attention on the critical task of configuring the simulator to reflect reality. We present iBox (“Internet in a Box”), which enables data-driven network path simulation, using input/output packet traces gathered at the sender/receiver in the target network to create a model of the end-to-end behaviour of a network path. Our work builds on recent work in this direction and makes three contributions: (1) estimation of a lightweight non-reactive cross-traffic model, (2) estimation of a more powerful reactive cross-traffic model based on Bayesian optimization, and (3) evaluation of iBox in the context of congestion control variants in an Internet research testbed and also controlled experiments with known ground truth.
title: Data-Driven Network Path Simulation with iBox

summary: In software-defined networking (SDN), the decoupled architecture provides opportunities for efficiently measuring critical quality of service (QoS) parameters, such as delay. Existing approaches, to dynamically obtain delay, are based around calculating the transit time of a probe packet that travels through the data links. These approaches are not efficient as the probe packet injected into the data plane incurs considerable overhead. Additionally, a separate probe packet is required to measure the delay of each queue if more than one queue is present on the egress port of a switch. Thus, these approaches are not scalable. In this paper, we propose an efficient passive delay estimation method, queueing delay monitoring (qMon), to monitor queueing delay in SDN networks. qMon leverages the OpenFlow protocol to obtain queue statistics from switches at regular intervals, which are further employed to estimate the mean queueing delay for each interval. Thus, the proposed approach differs from the existing approaches as no packet is injected into the data plane to measure delay. The results show that for Poisson traffic and for bursty traffic with large ON intervals, round trip time (RTT) values estimated using qMon and ping utility demonstrate high correlation when the measured RTT value is considered as time-series data.
title: qMon: A method to monitor queueing delay in OpenFlow networks

content:
Link to source code: qdisc_stats
Receiving the netlink reply In the last part of this article, we learnt how to create a netlink request message for queueing discipline stats and send it. This post focuses on the next part, that is, receiving the reply to our request. Receiving netlink request reply is just the reverse of creating a request message and sending it. We will use the same structs to parse our messages, as we did while sending the request, along with a few more structs to parse stats.
To receive the message we will first peak over the message, using MSG_PEEK flag (input buffer will regard it as message not read and therefore will not discard it.), to determine its length and then allocate a buffer for it accordingly. This is to ensure optimal memory usage. The subsequent message read will then copy the contents of the message into the buffer allocated from the input buffer. (The message will be then be discarded from the input buffer). As we did with sendmsg, we will use struct msghdr to receive the message. We will also need one additional flag MSG_TRUNC. This flag indicates that if buffer size was insufficient for receiving the data, then how much of the data was truncated. While initializing struct msghdr, we set the iovrecv (buffer) to point to NULL, so that all of the data is truncated, and the size of the data is returned by recvmsg().
/* Read message from the kernel */ struct sockaddr_nl nladdr; struct iovec iovrecv; iovrecv.iov_base = NULL; iovrecv.iov_len = 0; struct msghdr msg_recv = { .msg_name = &nladdr, .msg_namelen = sizeof(nladdr), .msg_iov = &iovrecv, .msg_iovlen = 1, }; char *buf; /* Determine buffer length for receiving the message */ int recvlen = recvmsg(sock_fd, &msg_recv, MSG_PEEK | MSG_TRUNC); if (recvlen < 32768) recvlen = 32768;  recvlen is the size of the memory that we need to receive the message. We allocate a buffer of size recvlen accordingly and initialize msg_iov with it.
buf = malloc(recvlen); /* Reset the I/O vector */ iovrecv.iov_base = buf; iovrecv.iov_len = recvlen; /* Now receive the message */ recvlen = recvmsg(sock_fd, &msg_recv, 0);  Next step: parse the received message!
Parsing the netlink reply RTNETLINK messages are parsed using macros defined in linux/rtnetlink.h (RTA_*) and linux/netlink.h (NLMSG_*). For better understanding, it is suggested that the reader goes through the definition of these macros while reading this article. Macros which we are going to use for parsing the message are summarized below:
 NLMSG_DATA(h) - defines access of netlink message payload, given pointer to netlink message header h (struct nlmsghdr *). For us, message payload is defined by struct tcmsg. NLMSG_OK(h, msglen) - boolean value, whether message length corresponding to the header is OK. NLMSG_NEXT(nlh, len) - defines access of the netlink message (nlmsghdr) following the current message. It is assigned to a pointer of type struct nlmsghdr. TCA_RTA(tcrecv) - defines access of Routing attributes, given pointer to TC message payload (struct tcmsg). RTA_NEXT(rta, len) - defines access of next Routing attribute, given pointer to current rta (struct rtattr *). RTA_OK(rta, len) - boolean value, whether message length corresponding to the current routing attribute is OK. RTA_DATA(rta) - defines access to data corresponding to the attribute header rta (struct rtattr). Using these macros, we loop through the netlink messages. For every netlink message, we loop through family headers which define queueing discipline, that is struct tcmsg. For each family header, we parse the stats received as attributes. These attributes (struct rtattr) define the data as TLV (type, length value). If you don’t fully understand how these macros work, don’t worry. Reading the code will make it clear.  struct rtattr { unsigned short	rta_len; unsigned short	rta_type; /* Data follows */ };  Data might also be in a nested form. That isn’t a problem because we know what kind of data we are expected beforehand. So depending on the message type we would know whether data is present in nested form or not. In our case, data is present in nested form, as we will see later in the post. For now, lets iterate through the netlink messages and parse each message (nl_print_qdisc_stats()):
while (NLMSG_OK(h, msglen)) { printf ("\n -------------- \n"); if (h->nlmsg_type == NLMSG_DONE) { printf("\nDone iterating through the netlink messages"); break; } if (h->nlmsg_type == NLMSG_ERROR) { printf("\nError message encountered!"); break; } /* Parse this message */ /* move to next message */ h = NLMSG_NEXT(h, msglen); }  For each netlink message, we get the family header using NLMSG_DATA(h):
struct tcmsg *tcrecv = NLMSG_DATA(h);  From the family header, we fetch the TLVs (type length values in form of (struct rtattr)(rta) + (void *)(data))
struct rtattr *rta = TCA_RTA(tcrecv);  We then loop through the TLVs, which we expect to be TCA_MAX + 1 to be in number, and copy them to struct rtattr tb[] in the corresponding type …
summary: A tutorial with code on how to fetch queueing discipline stats from kernel using RTNETLINK.
title: RTNETLINK 2: Fetching queueing discipline stats

summary: A tutorial with code on how to fetch queueing discipline stats from kernel using RTNETLINK.
title: RTNETLINK 1: Fetching queueing discipline stats
